title: Relative position bias
description: Attention logits adjusted by a learned or fixed bias that depends on distance between query and key positions instead of absolute index vectors.
openingSummary: Relative position bias adds a distance-dependent term to attention scores so the model encodes how far apart two tokens are without storing a separate absolute position vector per index.

whatItIs.title: What It Is
whatItIs.body: Relative position bias keeps token embeddings mostly position-agnostic and instead modifies attention logits using the offset between query index i and key index j. The bias may come from a learned bucket table, a handcrafted slope, or another distance function. Self-attention still uses standard Q/K/V projections; only the score matrix gains position-aware shifts.

whyItMatters.title: Why It Matters
whyItMatters.body: Encoder stacks, speech models, and some efficient attention variants prefer relative bias because the same distance bucket applies regardless of absolute location in a long stream. Reading ALiBi, T5, or sliding-window papers is easier once you separate relative bias schemes from absolute embedding tables or rotary transforms.

simpleExample.title: Simple Example
simpleExample.body: When token 10 attends to token 7, the model looks up the bias for relative distance −3 (or bucketed equivalent) and adds it to the dot product before softmax. Token 100 attending to token 97 reuses the same bias for the same separation.

commonConfusions.title: Common Confusions
commonConfusions.body: Relative bias is not ALiBi specifically—ALiBi is one linear slope recipe under this family. It is also not RoPE, which encodes distance by rotating Q/K rather than adding scalar bias. T5's bucketed table is a relative bias variant with its own bucketing rules, not interchangeable with every other RPE paper.
